title: The difference between healing and performing wellness
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
I spent years performing wellness before I knew what healing actually felt like 

 I had the morning routine down. Green smoothie. Journaling. Meditation app....
metaDescription:
I spent years performing wellness before I knew what healing actually felt like 

 I had the morning routine down. Green smoothie. Journaling. Meditation...
htmlContent:
I spent years performing wellness before I knew what healing actually felt like

I had the morning routine down. Green smoothie. Journaling. Meditation app. Yoga mat unrolled by 6:30 AM. I posted about it sometimes, not for validation, I told myself, but for accountability. I believed I was healing. I was doing everything right.

Here's the thing. I was also dissociating through most of it. My body was in the room, but I wasn't really there. I was performing a script I'd learned from Instagram influencers and wellness podcasts, not from my own nervous system. I was checking boxes, not actually changing anything underneath.

This is the difference that took me years to understand. Healing and performing wellness look almost identical from the outside. Same routines. Same vocabulary. Same crystals and breathwork and boundary-setting language. But on the inside, they feel completely different. One leaves you emptier. The other leaves you more real.

Stay with me here. Because if you've ever felt like you're doing all the right things but still feel broken inside, this article is for you.

Performing wellness is a survival strategy

Let me be direct about this. Performing wellness is often just another way to manage how others see you. It's a mask. A really sophisticated one that uses all the right buzzwords.

When you're performing wellness, you're focused on the appearance of healing. You want to look like someone who has their shit together. You want to be the person who has boundaries, who does the work, who radiates calm. And there's nothing wrong with wanting that. The problem is that the performance itself becomes a way to avoid what's actually happening inside.

I remember sitting in a meditation circle, eyes closed, breathing deeply, while inside I was screaming. I was using the meditation to not feel the grief that was waiting for me. I was performing presence to avoid presence. Wild, right?

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say that this kind of performance activates the social engagement system in a way that bypasses the ventral vagal state of true safety. You're smiling and breathing and saying the right things, but your nervous system knows the truth. It's still in defense mode. You're just hiding it better.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not in the story you tell about it. Performing wellness is often a story you tell. It's a narrative you construct to convince yourself and others that you're okay. But the body isn't convinced. The body knows the difference between a performed calm and a genuine settling of the nervous system.

Think about it. When you're performing, there's a tightness somewhere. Maybe in your jaw. Maybe in your shoulders. Maybe in that shallow breath that never quite reaches your belly. That's your body telling you something. That's the gap between performance and presence.

Healing is messy, slow, and often invisible

Healing doesn't look good on Instagram. It looks like crying in your car for no reason you can name. It looks like canceling plans because your nervous system is overwhelmed. It looks like sitting on the floor of your bathroom at 2 AM because a memory hit you and you couldn't stand up.

Healing is not linear. It's not a journey from broken to fixed. It's more like a spiral where you revisit the same wounds at different depths, each time with a little more capacity to be with them. Each time, you stay a little longer before you run.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," showed us that trauma isn't a story that happened in the past. It's a current reality in the body. The nervous system is still responding to threats that aren't there anymore. And healing means teaching that system, slowly, that it's safe now. That takes time. It takes repetition. It takes failing and trying again.

I spent six months working with a therapist who kept telling me to "feel my feelings." I thought I was. I was crying. I was journaling. I was doing the work. But one day she asked me a simple question: "Where do you feel that sadness in your body?" And I couldn't answer. I had no idea. I was feeling my feelings in my head, narrating them, analyzing them, but not actually inhabiting them.

That was the moment I realized I was performing healing. I was doing the external actions without the internal presence. The movements were right, but the nervous system wasn't along for the ride.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It helped me move from intellectual understanding to actual body-based practices. It's not about performing. It's about tracking what's actually happening in your system.

Healing requires a kind of honesty that performing doesn't. Performing says "I'm fine." Healing says "I'm not fine, and that's where I'm going to start." Performing reaches for the solution. Healing sits in the problem long enough to understand its shape.

How to tell the difference in your own body

Here's a question that changed everything for me: When you do a wellness practice, do you feel more alive or more numb? Do you feel more connected to yourself or more disconnected?

This isn't about feeling good. Healing often feels terrible. Grief feels terrible. Processing anger feels terrible. But there's a quality to it that's different from numbing. It's a terrible that feels real. It has texture. It has movement. It changes as you stay with it.

Performing wellness, on the other hand, feels flat. It feels like you're going through motions. It feels like you're trying to get somewhere else, somewhere better. There's a push behind it. A force. A "should."

I notice it in my body. When I'm performing, my breath is shallow and high in my chest. My shoulders are up near my ears. There's a sense of effort, of trying to make something happen. When I'm actually healing, my breath drops into my belly. My shoulders soften. There's a sense of allowing, not forcing. The effort is in staying present, not in changing anything.

Janina Fisher, who works with structural dissociation, talks about how parts of us can be invested in performing wellness while other parts are still terrified and hidden. You might have a part that's really good at meditation and boundary-setting and all the right language. But another part is still a child, frozen in time, waiting for someone to notice how scared it is. Performing wellness keeps that child hidden. Healing invites it out.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). But here's the catch. You can set boundaries as a performance too. You can say "no" in a way that's rigid and defended, or you can say "no" from a place of genuine self-knowledge. The difference is in the body. The difference is in whether you're saying no to control the other person or to protect your own nervous system.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did you tighten? Did you relax? Did you feel a pull toward defensiveness? That's information. That's your nervous system telling you something about where you are in this process.

The wellness industry wants you to perform

Let's be honest about something. The wellness industry makes money when you perform. It makes money when you buy the next course, the next supplement, the next retreat. It makes money when you believe that healing is something you can purchase, something you can achieve through the right combination of products and practices.

Real healing doesn't sell well. You can't market sitting in your pain. You can't monetize the slow, boring work of regulating your nervous system over years. You can't make a subscription model out of learning to be with your own grief.

I'm not saying all wellness products are bad. I've used books and workbooks and even apps that helped me. But the difference is in the relationship you have with them. Are you using them to avoid yourself or to meet yourself? Are you reaching for them because you want to feel different, or because you want to feel more?

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why I was so good at performing wellness in the first place. When you grow up having to manage your parents' emotions, you learn to perform. You learn to show up as the version of yourself that keeps everyone else comfortable. And then you bring that same skill into your healing. You become the "good patient," the "good meditator," the "good trauma survivor." But you're still not free.

Freedom comes when you stop performing. When you let yourself be the mess you actually are. When you let your nervous system move at its own pace, not the pace of your to-do list or your Instagram feed.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work helped me understand that the goal isn't to become a perfectly healed person. The goal is to become a person who can hold their own pain without collapsing. That's different. That's real.

The return is not a destination

I used to think healing was a place I would arrive at. A state of being where I would finally be okay. Where the triggers would stop. Where the flashbacks would end. Where I would feel whole and complete and never struggle again.

That's not how it works. That's a performance fantasy. That's the wellness industry's version of a happy ending.

Real healing is more like learning to live with yourself. It's learning to be with the parts of you that are broken and the parts that are whole, at the same time. It's learning that you don't have to be fixed to be loved. You don't have to be healed to be worthy.

The return, as we call it here at The Shattered Armor, is not a return to some original wholeness. It's a return to yourself. The self you've been hiding from. The self you've been performing over. The self that's been waiting, patiently, for you to stop pretending and just be here.

I still perform sometimes. I catch myself in a meditation and realize I'm just going through the motions. I catch myself in a conversation about boundaries and realize I'm using the language to sound evolved, not to actually protect myself. That's okay. That's part of it.

The difference now is that I notice. And when I notice, I can choose again. I can drop the performance. I can take a breath that actually reaches my belly. I can be the mess I am, right here, right now.

That's healing. Not the absence of performance. But the willingness to see it, name it, and come back to what's real.

You don't have to be good at this. You don't have to be consistent. You don't have to be anything other than what you are right now. That's the whole point. That's the return.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
